| Chile Flakes | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Category | Herbs and spices |

Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients  | Herbs and Spices

**Chile flakes**—also known as **chili flakes**, **red pepper flakes**, or **crushed red pepper**[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)—are a spice derived from dried and crushed red chile peppers. It should not be confused with either chili powder or chile powder.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

As the name implies, chile flakes are not fully ground, instead consisting of small chips or flakes. Some varieties, especially those labeled "crushed red pepper" or "red pepper flakes" may also contain the chile seeds along with the flakes.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) Chile flakes can also be made from either a single chile variety or from a blend[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)—jalapeño, serrano, and cayenne are common varieties included in blends.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) Gochugaru, Aleppo, and Sichuan are other specialty flake varieties.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[4]](#cite_note-bonappetit_rg-4)[[5]](#cite_note-thewoksoflife-5) The exact heat level and flavor profile will depend on the specific variety of chile used.[[4]](#cite_note-bonappetit_rg-4)

![](/wiki/File:Gochugaru.jpg)

Gochugaru

### Varieties

[edit | edit source]

The following include some specific varieties of chile flake and their characteristics:

* **"Crushed red pepper"/"pizza pepper":** Common in the USA; fairly hot; usually includes seeds; darker red and often roasted[[4]](#cite_note-bonappetit_rg-4)[[5]](#cite_note-thewoksoflife-5)
* **Gochugaru:** Korean chile flakes; bright red and coarse ground; no seeds; fruity and relatively mild flavor; used primarily in Korean dishes[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)
* **Aleppo pepper:** From the Halaby chile; relatively mild and earthy flavor; deep red color; somewhat oily texture; ideally seedless[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)
* **Sichuan chile flakes:** less hot than crushed red pepper but hotter than gochugaru; used in Chinese (especially Sichuan) cuisines[[5]](#cite_note-thewoksoflife-5)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Like all dried spices, chile flakes should be kept in an airtight container away from light, heat, and moisture. The flavor will gradually diminish over time, so buy only as much as you will use in a few months. They should smell pungent.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Chile flakes have a variety of applications. One common use is as a topping for pizzas, pastas, and salads,[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) where they add both heat and aesthetically pleasing red speckles.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-bonappetit_rg-4)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

If you can't access ready-made chile flakes, you can make your own by crushing or grinding dried red chile peppers.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) Since different chile varieties have different heat levels and flavor profiles, you'll want to add a little at a time when substituting, tasting to get the correct heat level.[[4]](#cite_note-bonappetit_rg-4)
